text:
TITLE: Generation X: We Were There for All of It
From Rabbit Ears to AI

I was born in May of 1969 and graduated high school in 1987, which means I got a front-row seat to one of the greatest cultural transitions in modern history.

I grew up in a suburb of Cleveland, Ohio, in a world that still had rabbit ears on televisions, handwritten notes, black-and-white screens, muscle cars, paper routes, corner gas stations, 8-track tapes, cassette players, arcades, malls, and kids who were told to go outside and come home when the streetlights came on.

And now here we are, living in a world of smartphones, streaming, social media, artificial intelligence, and technology moving so fast most people can barely keep up with it.

Generation X was there for all of it.

We were born into an analog world. A world where you had to get up and turn the knob on the TV. A world where your dad might tell you to adjust the rabbit ears, and when the picture finally came in clear, he would say, “Stand there for a while and hold it.” And we did. Because that was just life.

We had console TVs, black-and-white TVs, and only a handful of channels. We did not scroll through thousands of options. We watched what was on. If you missed your show, you missed it. There was no replay button, no streaming, no pause, no rewind unless you had a VCR later on and remembered to set the timer right.

Family vacations were different too. I remember those old vans from the late 70s and early 80s, the kind with the beds, bucket seats, TVs, and that full rolling living room feel. My dad would play 8-track tapes while we drove for hours. Kenny Rogers, The Gambler, Dolly Parton, all of it playing inside that van for what felt like twelve straight hours. And somehow, that was part of the magic.

We were latchkey kids before anybody turned it into a label. A lot of us came home from school to an empty house because both parents were working. We let ourselves in, made something to eat, watched TV, went outside, did our homework if we felt like being responsible, and waited for our parents to get home. There was trust back then. Maybe not perfect trust, but real trust. Parents expected you to use your head. They expected you to survive a few hours without constant supervision.

I can remember being nine or ten years old and walking to the Sunoco gas station on the corner of my street. My mother smoked back then, though she does not anymore, and she would send me with a couple dollars and a note. The note would say something like, “This is Bruce’s mom. Please sell him a pack of Winstons.” And the cashier knew us. He knew the family. He knew the neighborhood. So he sold them to me, and I walked back home.

Today, people would lose their minds over something like that. But back then, that was the world we lived in. The neighborhood had a kind of common sense to it. People knew each other. Kids were trusted with small responsibilities. We were sent on errands. We walked places. We figured things out. And somehow, without tracking apps, cell phones, cameras, or constant hovering, most of us made it home just fine.

We drank out of hoses. We rolled around in dirt. We accidentally ate dirt once in a while and survived. We rode bikes all over the neighborhood. Our parents did not panic if they could not see us every second of the day. They kicked us outside and said, “Come back when the streetlights come on.” And we did.

That kind of freedom built something in us.

It also taught us how to work. I started cutting grass and shoveling driveways when I was still a kid. I had customers every summer. I had paper routes. At one point, I had two paper routes. I had money in my pocket because I earned it. I did not need a handout, and I was proud of that.

That is another thing about Generation X that sometimes gets overlooked. We were free, but we were also expected to be responsible. Nobody was handing us everything. We learned early how to work, earn, save, and stand on our own two feet. We bought our own things. We saved for cars. We paid for concerts. We spent our money on gas, music, dates, weekends, and whatever freedom looked like at the time.

And then came the 80s.

For me, the 80s were magical. I loved everything about them. The colors. The fashion. The music. The movies. The cars. The attitude. The birth of cable television. The birth of MTV. The glitz, the glamour, the hair, the lights, the energy. It was larger than life.

Miami Vice was huge for me. Still to this day, it is my favorite show. The look, the music, the style, the whole feeling of it captured something about that era that is hard to explain to someone who did not live through it.

The malls were incredible. The arcades were incredible. You could walk through a mall and feel like the whole world was alive. Music was playing. Teenagers were everywhere. Stores had personality. People were actually looking at each other, talking to each other, flirting with each other, bumping into friends, making plans, killing time, and living life in real space.

Then there were the arcades. The sound of those machines, the quarters lined up, the lights flashing, the competition, the crowd around a game when somebody was good. That was our version of online gaming, except we were standing next to each other. You had to look people in the eye. You had to talk. You had to be there.

Music was everything. I became a rock and roll metalhead guy. I went to concerts constantly. Back then, there might be one or two concerts a week, and you could actually afford to go. Tickets were fifteen or twenty dollars. That sounds impossible now, but it was real. I saw Metallica. I saw Ozzy. I saw so many bands. The concert scene back then was unbelievable.

And the cars. Man, the cars.

My first car was a 1972 Cutlass Supreme with a 350 four-barrel, a racing stripe, bucket seats, white leather interior, raised white letters on the tires, and Cragar rims. That was cool. Back then, a lot of my friends had muscle cars or souped-up cars. Cars were part of your identity. They were freedom. They were status. They were how you got around, how you picked up your friends, how you took a girl out, how you showed a little personality before social media ever existed.

And yes, the girls loved it.

That was part of the time too. Flirting was different. Dating was different. Social life was different. It was face to face. It was awkward sometimes. It was funny sometimes. It was nervous, bold, embarrassing, exciting, and real. Boys chased girls. Girls flirted back. We learned how to talk to each other, how to read a room, how to take a chance, and how to handle rejection without making a public spectacle out of it.

Today, so much of that has been replaced by screens, suspicion, social media performance, and people being angry about almost everything. I am not saying everything back then was perfect. It was not. But I am saying something about it was deeply alive.

That is one of the things I miss most.

I met my high school sweetheart in the 80s. She went to the local public high school, and I went to the local Catholic school. I only went to that Catholic school because they had a superior hockey team. But my sister went to the public school, and that is how I was introduced to the girl who would one day become my wife.

That alone feels like something from another world now.

We passed notes through my sister. I would write a note, give it to my sister, and she would take it to school. Then she would come home with a note from her. There was anticipation in that. You had to wait. You had to wonder. You had to put thought into what you wrote. There was no instant reply, no typing dots, no read receipt, no social media stalking. Just a note, a little hope, and the wait.

I miss that.

There was something magical about courtship back then. You had to make an effort. You had to call the house phone and hope her father did not answer. You had to show up. You had to drive over. You had to talk. You had to risk being nervous. There was no hiding behind a screen.

One of the memories I will carry with me forever happened in November of 1987, not long after I started dating the girl who would become my wife. Our first concert together was Bon Jovi on the Slippery When Wet tour, with Cinderella opening up. That night, I fell in love with Cinderella too. Tom Keifer walked out there with that voice, that grit, that whole 80s rock and roll attitude, and I was hooked. But more than anything, that was the night I think I truly knew I was falling in love with her.

She looked like she had stepped straight out of an MTV video. Bleached blonde hair, black boots, leopard spandex pants, pure 80s attitude and beauty. She had that look, that confidence, that spark. She was the kind of girl you did not forget. We were young, the music was loud, the lights were bright, and for a few hours, the world felt like it belonged to us.

Then Bon Jovi finished the show and warned everybody to be careful going home because it was bad outside. We walked out and Cleveland was buried in a blizzard. What should have been a thirty-minute drive turned into almost four hours.

To make it even better, I got a flat tire coming out of the parking lot. I had no gloves, my hands were freezing, and there I was trying to change a tire in the middle of a snowstorm. So my girlfriend, who would one day become my wife, handed me her leopard spandex pants so I could wrap them around my hands and grip the tire iron.

That is the kind of thing you never forget.

We finally made it home around four in the morning, and my father was sitting in his chair waiting for me, mad as could be. Looking back now, I can laugh about it. At the time, it was just another wild, unforgettable night in the 80s.

That was the magic of that era. The music, the weather, the cars, the clothes, the danger, the laughter, the love. Nothing was convenient, but everything felt alive.

Right out of high school, and even during my senior year, I threw some legendary parties. Some of the best nights of my life were not planned by an algorithm, promoted by an app, or captured for social media. They happened in garages, basements, driveways, and backyards.

We would stay up late in the garage, a bunch of friends laughing, drinking, telling stories, and playing air guitar on hockey sticks like we were on stage in front of thousands. In somebody’s basement, there might be neon posters on the wall, lava lamps glowing in the corner, and a stereo pushing Pink Floyd or Led Zeppelin through the room. That was enough.

We did not need much more than music, friends, a weekend, and a place to gather.

I do not look back on those nights as something dark or shameful. They were real. They were part of growing up in that time. We gathered, we laughed, we listened to music, we had a few drinks, we flirted, we acted young, and then Monday morning came. We got up, went to work, and did what we had to do.

That was part of the code. You could have a legendary Saturday night and still show up Monday morning. We worked hard. We played hard. We understood that life had a rhythm.

We were not fragile. We were not helpless. We were learning how to become adults in a world that expected us to figure things out.

That is why I say Generation X was built different.

We saw the old world before it disappeared. We saw the birth of cable television. We saw MTV change music forever. We saw video games go from Pong to Atari to everything that came after. We saw 8-tracks fade into cassettes, cassettes fade into CDs, CDs fade into downloads, and downloads fade into streaming. We saw landlines give way to cell phones, cell phones give way to smartphones, and smartphones turn into the center of modern life.

We saw the internet arrive. We saw social media change everything. And now we are watching artificial intelligence step onto the world stage.

That is a rare thing in history.

There are younger people today who will never know what it was like to grow up without a phone in their hand. They will never know what it felt like to wait for a song to come on the radio so you could hit record on a cassette player. They will never know what it felt like to stand in a video store on a Friday night trying to pick the perfect movie. They will never know what it felt like to call a girl’s house and have to speak respectfully to whoever answered the phone.

They will never know what it felt like to be unreachable for a few hours and have that be normal.

I do not say that with hatred toward the younger generation. I say it with sadness, because there was something valuable in that kind of life. There was a kind of freedom that cannot be downloaded. There was a kind of connection that cannot be replaced by likes, comments, and notifications.

At the same time, I am not blind to the good that exists today.

Technology has brought incredible things into our lives. It has allowed people to create, connect, learn, build businesses, share stories, and reach audiences they never could have reached before. I know that personally, because I am living it now.

I went to broadcasting and television production school around 1990 and graduated at the top of my class. My dream was always to work in broadcasting, television, and film. I wanted to produce, direct, write, create, and tell stories. Over the years, I got to work in a lot of those areas. I produced. I directed. I wrote. I worked live events, sports, concerts, and different forms of media.

But like a lot of people, life did not move in a straight line.

Now, all these years later, I find myself standing at the edge of the dream I carried for most of my life. I may be a late bloomer, but I am still blooming. The same technology that has changed the world so dramatically is now allowing me to do things I could not have done back then. It has given me tools, reach, creative power, and a real community of people online who support what I am building. That part of this new world is beautiful.

I do not hate technology. I do not fear artificial intelligence. I have lived from rabbit ears to AI, and I see both sides of it. I know what we lost, but I also see what can still be gained. I am writing about AI now because I believe it can be used for good. I believe it can help people create, think, learn, communicate, and build things that matter.

Like anything else, it depends on the heart and mind of the person using it.

I have lived through a lot. I lost my best friend in 1992, just months before my wedding. He was supposed to be my best man, but he died in a car accident while at Akron University. I lost my younger sister when she was still in her early thirties. That was not supposed to happen. And then I lost my wife, my high school sweetheart, the girl from those handwritten notes and that Bon Jovi concert, a little over four years ago.

I have also walked through addiction, alcoholism, drugs, trouble with the law, rehabs, failures, grief, shame, and years of trying to find my way back. I have been to hell and back. But by the grace of God, I am still here.

I do not hide from my past. I am not ashamed of what I overcame. I am grateful. God carried me when I could not carry myself, and He gave me another day.

So when I look at this new world, I do not look at it as a defeated man. I look at it as someone who has been through enough to know what matters. I have seen the old world. I have survived the hard parts of this one. And as long as I have breath in my lungs, I am going to use whatever tools God puts in my hands to put good into the world.

Generation X was there for all of it.

We were there for rabbit ears, black-and-white TVs, 8-tracks, cassettes, Atari, arcades, MTV, malls, muscle cars, handwritten love notes, pay phones, cable television, the internet, smartphones, social media, and now artificial intelligence.

We were the last kids of the old world and the first adults of the new one.

And now, after everything I have lived through, I am still here.

Still creating.

Still believing.

Still using my voice.

Still putting good into the world.

Written by Bruce Snyder
Founder, Mindfix Entertainment
